food_subgroup: Chickpea and tahini dip; low-GI (~28); high MUFA/PUFA fat from tahini and olive oil

ontology.foodex2_description: Hummus bi tahini (chickpea dip with tahini)

serving.description: 3 tablespoons (~75g) — standard Singapore restaurant mezze portion
serving.natural_language_portion: A standard Singapore restaurant mezze serving of hummus is approximately 3 tablespoons (75g), providing around 178 kcal, 5.9g protein, 10.7g fat, and 13.2g carbohydrate with 3.4g dietary fibre.

nutrient_source.title: Hummus — USDA SR Legacy (FDC ID 172442)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: USDA SR Legacy FDC ID 172442 is a direct match for hummus (commercial, prepared from chickpeas and tahini). All per-100g nutrient values are sourced from this entry. Confidence: high.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values — hummus (chickpeas + tahini)
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI ~28 (LOW) sourced from University of Sydney GI Database for hummus (chickpeas + tahini). GL per 75g serving calculated as: (28 × 17.6g carbohydrate per 100g × 0.75) / 100 = ~3.7; however the input record documents GL ~2.8 based on available carbohydrate (net carbs after fibre), consistent with published values. GL 2.8 retained as per input verified value. Confidence: 0.9.

hypertension.key_factor: Moderate sodium (~210mg per 75g serving); high potassium (228mg/100g); MUFA/PUFA fat profile supports vascular health
hypertension.guidance: At ~210mg sodium per 75g serving, hummus is a low-sodium dip suitable for hypertensive individuals; the potassium content (228mg/100g) and MUFA/PUFA fat profile from tahini and olive oil further support blood pressure management, though individuals should be aware that commercial brands may contain up to 400mg sodium per 75g.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low GI (~28); GL ~2.8 per 75g serving; 4.5g dietary fibre per 100g reduces postprandial glucose response
type2_diabetes.guidance: Hummus has a very low glycaemic index (~28) and glycaemic load (~2.8 per 75g serving), making it one of the most diabetes-friendly dips available; the chickpea soluble fibre (4.5g/100g) slows glucose absorption and regular consumption is associated with improved glycaemic control in Type 2 Diabetes.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Chickpea soluble fibre reduces LDL cholesterol; MUFA/PUFA fat from tahini and olive oil is anti-inflammatory; zero trans fat; low saturated fat (1.9g/100g)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Hummus is cardioprotective: chickpea soluble fibre (beta-glucans, pectin) reduces LDL cholesterol, the MUFA/PUFA fat profile from tahini (linoleic acid) and olive oil (oleic acid) is anti-inflammatory, saturated fat is low at 1.9g/100g, and trans fat is zero — making hummus a strongly recommended dip for individuals managing cardiovascular disease risk.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory MUFA/PUFA fats; chickpea soluble fibre supports gut microbiome; garlic allicin; folate 55mcg/100g
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Hummus is broadly positive for rheumatoid arthritis: chickpea soluble fibre supports anti-inflammatory gut microbiome activity, tahini PUFA (linoleic acid) and olive oil MUFA (oleic acid) are anti-inflammatory, garlic allicin has Grade B anti-inflammatory evidence, and folate at 55mcg/100g is clinically meaningful for individuals on methotrexate therapy.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Hummus (Lebanese-style) is a Levantine chickpea dip made by blending cooked chickpeas (garbanzo beans) with tahini (sesame seed paste), fresh lemon juice, garlic, olive oil, and salt until smooth and creamy. It is one of the oldest known prepared foods in the Middle East, originating in the Levant region (Lebanon, Syria, Israel, Palestinian Territories). The texture is smooth and velvety with a nutty, slightly tangy flavour from the tahini and lemon. In Singapore, hummus is served as a mezze starter at Middle Eastern and Mediterranean restaurants including Zaatar W Zeit, Cedar Lebanese, and Al Shams. A standard Singapore restaurant mezze portion is 3 tablespoons (~75g), providing approximately 178 kcal, 5.9g protein, 10.7g fat (predominantly MUFA and PUFA from tahini and olive oil), 13.2g carbohydrate, and 3.4g dietary fibre. Per 100g: 237 kcal, 7.9g protein, 14.3g fat, 17.6g carbohydrate, 4.5g dietary fibre, 280mg sodium. Glycaemic index is very low (~28, LOW category) with a glycaemic load of ~2.8 per 75g serving — one of the lowest-GI dips available. Clinically, hummus is beneficial for Type 2 Diabetes (very low GI, chickpea fibre reduces postprandial glucose), hypertension (low sodium, high potassium), and cardiovascular disease (LDL-lowering soluble fibre, anti-inflammatory MUFA/PUFA fat profile, zero trans fat). The food is vegan, gluten-free, dairy-free, and halal. Primary nutrient source: USDA SR Legacy FDC ID 172442.
common_user_queries: is hummus good for high blood pressure

confidence_reason: All per-100g nutrient values sourced directly from USDA SR Legacy FDC ID 172442 (exact match for hummus prepared from chickpeas and tahini). GI value sourced from University of Sydney GI Database. All core macros, secondary nutrients, GI, and ontology codes are fully supported by approved primary sources.
notes: trans_fat_g is confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy FDC 172442 — hummus contains no hydrogenated oils. cholesterol_mg is confirmed zero as this is a plant-based food with no animal-derived ingredients. vitamin_b12_mcg is confirmed zero as this is a vegan food with no animal-derived ingredients. vitamin_d_mcg is confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy FDC 172442. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown not separately reported in the primary USDA SR Legacy FDC 172442 entry used; these values are present in some USDA entries but were not available in the specific record accessed. water_g set to -1: moisture content not reported in the primary source entry accessed. GL per serving calculated using available carbohydrate method consistent with published University of Sydney GL values for hummus (~2.8 per 75g). This record covers Lebanese-style restaurant hummus as served in Singapore mezze portions; commercial packaged hummus may have higher sodium (up to 400mg per 75g).
